data.content: I'll create the `Directory.Packages.props` file and update the `.csproj` to remove inline versions.

arguments.file_text:
<Project>
  <PropertyGroup>
    <ManagePackageVersionsCentrally>true</ManagePackageVersionsCentrally>
  </PropertyGroup>
  <ItemGroup>
    <PackageVersion Include="Microsoft.Extensions.Logging" Version="9.0.0" />
    <PackageVersion Include="System.Text.Json" Version="9.0.0" />
    <PackageVersion Include="Polly" Version="8.5.0" />
  </ItemGroup>
</Project>

arguments.old_str:
<PackageReference Include="Microsoft.Extensions.Logging" Version="9.0.0" />
    <PackageReference Include="System.Text.Json" Version="9.0.0" />
    <PackageReference Include="Polly" Version="8.5.0" />

data.reasoningText:
Now I need to:
1. Create a `Directory.Packages.props` file at the root (or at the `simple-single-project` level) that centralizes the package versions
2. Remove the `Version` attributes from the `PackageReference` elements in the `.csproj` file

The `Directory.Packages.props` should be placed at the `simple-single-project` directory level (the solution/project root).

arguments.file_text:
<Project>

    <ManagePackageVersionsCentrally>true</ManagePackageVersionsCentrally>
  </PropertyGroup>
  <ItemGroup>
    <PackageVersion Include="Microsoft.Extensions.Logging" Version="9.0.0" />
    <PackageVersion Include="System.Text.Json" Version="9.0.0" />
    <PackageVersion Include="Polly" Version="8.5.0" />
  </ItemGroup>
</Project>

data.content:
Done. Here's what I did:

1. **Created `simple-single-project/Directory.Packages.props`** — enables CPM and declares all package versions centrally using `<PackageVersion>` items.
2. **Updated `MyApp.csproj`** — removed `Version` attributes from all `<PackageReference>` elements, so versions are now controlled by the props file.

The three packages (`Microsoft.Extensions.Logging`, `System.Text.Json`, `Polly`) are now centrally managed.
